landscapeTheme: Beach / Shopping Center â€” casual surface, serious business underneath

scenes.heading: Meetings â€” Time Is the Currency
scenes.narration: The beach may look relaxed, but the currents keep moving. The same is true for U.S. business meetings. The tone feels casual, but the clock is always running. A Peruvian founder joined a thirty-minute introductory call three minutes late. The American was wrapping up by minute twenty-five. The founder never got a second call. Three minutes. That is all it took. In U.S. business, time is the currency, and being late is like showing up to a meeting having already spent some of theirs. Let me walk you through exactly how to handle meetings, negotiation, and networking the way Americans expect.

scenes.heading: Before, During, and After the Meeting
bullets: Before: send a three-bullet agenda and confirm the day before
During: start on time, stay on topic, end with clear action items
After: send a recap email within twenty-four hours â€” what was discussed and next steps
Default to thirty minutes â€” asking for an hour signals you do not value their time
scenes.narration: Before the meeting, send a three-bullet agenda by email. It shows you respect their time. Confirm the day before with a quick note: looking forward to our call tomorrow at two PM Eastern. Be ready five minutes early. During the meeting, start on time. One to two minutes of small talk is normal. Five minutes feels wasteful. Stay on the agenda. Americans value agenda-driven meetings. Tangents are politely tolerated but internally noted. End with clear action items: who does what, by when. After the meeting, send a recap email within twenty-four hours. Include what was discussed, what was agreed, next steps, and deadlines. In U.S. business, if it is not in writing, it did not happen. Default to thirty minutes for first meetings. Asking for an hour from someone you have never met signals that you do not value their schedule.

scenes.heading: Negotiation â€” More Direct Than You Expect
bullets: Both sides state their position early â€” vague responses signal you have not done your homework
Expect one to two rounds of counter-offers â€” prolonged haggling signals distrust
First offers are close to final â€” an offer of forty-five might settle at forty-two, not thirty
Nothing is real until it is in a signed contract or detailed email
scenes.narration: A Mexican SaaS founder spent three meetings building rapport before discussing terms. After the third, his American contact asked: so, what is the number? He was not ready with pricing. The deal stalled. U.S. negotiation moves fast. Both sides state their position early. Vague responses like we are flexible signal you have not done your homework. Expect one to two rounds of counter-offers. Prolonged haggling is perceived as distrust. First offers are close to final. If someone offers forty-five thousand, they might settle at forty-two. Expecting thirty will end the conversation. And nothing is real until it is in a signed contract or detailed email. Written terms always matter more than handshakes.

scenes.heading: Networking â€” Quality Over Quantity
bullets: You have sixty seconds to explain who you are and what you do
People speak with you five to ten minutes and move on â€” the goal is initial connection
Follow up within forty-eight hours with something specific from the conversation
Give before you ask: share an article, make an introduction, offer an insight
scenes.narration: Networking events are the shoreline where everyone mingles. Americans speak with you for five to ten minutes and move on. The goal is initial connections, not closing deals. You have sixty seconds to explain who you are and what you do. Practice your elevator pitch until it flows naturally. Three networking rules founders miss. First, follow up within forty-eight hours. Reference something specific: great talking about supply chain logistics beats nice to meet you. Second, ask before you pitch. Read the conversation. Ask about their business first. If there is a fit, briefly describe what you do. Third, give before you ask. Share an article, make an introduction, offer an insight. The best connections form gradually, through steady deposits over time.

scenes.heading: The Follow-Through Test
scenes.narration: Here is something most founders do not realize. Americans test new business partners with small commitments. Send me that by Friday. Can you get me three references? Share your company deck before our next call. Each of these is a quiet test. Deliver on time and you move forward. Miss the deadline, and the relationship stalls without anyone telling you why. Every small commitment you keep builds trust for the bigger ones. A U.S. contact will not say you lost my trust because you sent the proposal late. They will simply stop responding. The test is invisible. But the results are absolute. Treat every small promise like a contract, because in U.S. business culture, it effectively is one.

scenes.heading: The Recap Email Template
bullets: Keep it brief: five to eight lines maximum
What was discussed, what was agreed, next steps with specific deadlines
Example: you will send the vendor application by Wednesday, I will have pricing by Friday
Creates a paper trail that protects both sides
scenes.narration: Let me give you a practical tool you can use after every meeting. The recap email. Keep it brief â€” five to eight lines. State what was discussed, what was agreed, and next steps with specific deadlines. Here is an example: great speaking today. To recap, you will send the vendor application by Wednesday, and I will have our pricing sheet to you by Friday. Let me know if I missed anything. This email does three things. It confirms alignment so there are no misunderstandings. It creates a paper trail that protects you if someone later claims they did not agree to something. And it signals professionalism. Make this a habit after every meeting and your credibility will compound.

scenes.heading: Every Commitment Counts
scenes.narration: Be on time, stay focused on the agenda, and follow up in writing within twenty-four hours. U.S. negotiation moves fast â€” come prepared with specific numbers and expect one to two rounds. At networking events, listen first, pitch second, and always follow up the next day. Every small commitment you keep builds trust for the bigger ones. Learn to read the currents beneath the casual surface, and you will navigate U.S. business relationships with confidence. Next lesson: the cultural missteps that cost real money â€” the undertow that pulls you under when you least expect it.
